title: NA-ULIP
word_text:
Child investment plans
As parents, you might want to provide the best for your children, whether it is clothes, food, or education. That is why parents try to save for their children’s future, whether it is for higher education, career, or marriage. Child investment plans help parents to save for their children’s future and create a corpus.
Different types of child investment plans are available in the market that parents can choose from. Each investment plan has its own set of features and benefits. Let’s explore.
Best Investment Options for Your Child’s Future
Here is a list of some common child investment plan options for parents -
Unit-Linked Insurance Plans
Called ULIPs in short, unit-linked plans are life insurance plans which combine the dual benefits of insurance coverage and returns that are linked to market performance. A part of the premium that you pay for the ULIP is used to provide insurance coverage, while the other part is invested in a market-linked fund.
ULIPs help you create a corpus for your child’s future over the long term and offer the potential of attractive returns. You also get the flexibility of switching and partial withdrawals to manage your investment.
Life Insurance
Besides ULIPs, endowment and money-back plans also help you create a corpus for your child’s future. You can choose child insurance plans that offer the added protection of a premium waiver rider. This rider waives the premiums if the parent passes away during the policy tenure. However, the policy continues and helps in creating the corpus even in the parents’ absence.
Systematic Investment Plans (SIPs)
Systematic Investment Plans (SIPs) are modes of investing in mutual funds. SIPs allow you to invest systematically and in a disciplined manner without the need to time the market. Moreover, they are an affordable way to start building a corpus for your child over time.
Fixed or Recurring Deposits
You can also choose fixed or recurring deposit schemes to create a guaranteed corpus for your child. Under fixed deposits, you deposit a lump sum amount of money for a specific tenure at a specified interest rate. Under recurring deposits, you invest a specific sum of money at regular intervals and up to a specified tenure.
Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (SSY)
The SSY scheme is a savings scheme for the girl child that is backed by the government. The scheme helps you create a corpus for your daughter through regular contributions. The interest rate is fixed and determined by the government from time to time.
Gold
You can also invest in physical or digital gold to save up for your child’s future. Gold prices usually rise over time, allowing you to enjoy returns on your investment. Moreover, gold as an asset can also be used for its resale or traditional value.
Public Provident Fund (PPF)
PPF is another government-backed scheme that offers a guaranteed income on your savings. You can start investing in the PPF scheme from as little as ₹100. The maximum deposit is limited to ₹1.5 lakhs, and PPFs come with a tenure of 15 years,s which can be increased in blocks of 5 years.
Bonds
Bonds are debt instruments used by corporations or the government to raise funds. You can invest in bonds and earn coupon payments (interest income) on the amount invested. Many bonds are also listed on the stock exchange, offering liquidity.
Real Estate
You can also invest in property to save for your children. Property values also appreciate over time. When needed, you can sell the property to get a lump sum amount that can be used to fund your child’s education or any other financial need. Moreover, you can let out the property and generate a regular source of income through rent.
Mutual Funds
Mutual funds are pooled investment avenues that offer market-linked returns on investments. There are different types of mutual fund schemes like equity funds, debt funds, hybrid funds, etc. You can choose a scheme that matches your investment strategy and risk appetite.
Debt Funds
Debt funds are a type of mutual fund that invest primarily in debt instruments like fixed deposits, bonds, government securities, etc. Debt funds are suitable for investors with a low risk profile and those who want stable returns on their investment.
How do child savings plans work?
You can assess and choose a suitable child savings plan depending on your needs, risk appetite, investment horizon, and other factors. Then, you can invest in the child plan in a lump sum or systematically, depending on the type of investment avenue selected and your savings preference.
Your investment would grow with time. Once the investment tenure is over, you can use the accumulated corpus for your child’s needs.
Best Time to Start a Child Investment Plan
When starting to save for your child, here are some things to consider -
Early Start
When saving for your child, it is better to start early. This would give you the benefit of compounding returns. Compounding means earning interest on the already earned interest. Over a period of time, this can help you earn exponential returns on your savings.
When you start early, you can give your investment time to grow, and compounding can help enhance the returns.
Birth or Early Childhood
You can also start investing after your child is born or when your child is young. This would give you time to save up a corpus for your child’s higher education or marriage.
Stable Financial Situation
Assess your finances, too, when starting investments for your child. Saving is feasible when your financial situation is stable so that the saving doesn’t affect your budget.
Benefits of Child Investment Plans
Some of the benefits of child investment plans are as follows -
Rising Education Costs
Education costs are rising steadily. You would need to create an optimal fund for your child’s higher education, whether in India or abroad. Child investment plans help you create this corpus over time through savings. The different types of child investment plans can generate returns on your savings and help you create funds for your child’s education.
Financial Security
Life insurance child plans add the element of financial security to savings. Child plans usually offer the premium waiver benefit feature. This benefit secures the child’s future even if the parent passes away during the policy tenure. In the parents’ absence, the continuity of the child plan is not affected. The plan continues till maturity and pays the maturity benefit, which can help the child meet their financial needs.
Inflation Hedge
Inflation reduces the purchasing power of money over time. The corpus required for your child’s education or marriage can increase with time. When you choose child investment plans that offer inflation-beating returns, you can create a corpus that would give your child the funds needed to meet the inflated expenses.
Compounding Benefits
Child investment plans that offer compounded returns can help grow your savings exponentially over time. You can create an optimal corpus with the benefit of compounding to meet the costs of education or marriage.
Features of Child Investment Plans
Investment and Protection
Sum Received Upon Maturity
Partial Withdrawals
Emergency Reserves
How Much to Invest Monthly for Your Child’s Future Goals?
The investment amount depends on your savings and the corpus that you need for your child’s future. Here are some instances showing how much you need to save for different combinations of interest rate, investment period, and the corpus needed –
Select the Best Investment Plan for Your Child
When choosing an investment plan for your child, here are some factors to consider -
Risk Profile
Assess your risk-taking ability so that you can choose a suitable investment avenue. For instance, if you don’t mind taking investment risks, you can invest in market-linked avenues like mutual funds, ULIPs, stocks, etc. However, if you do not like taking risks, you can choose fixed-income avenues like fixed deposits, recurring deposits, etc.
Investment Horizon
Next, consider your investment horizon, i.e., the period you have to invest after which you would need the corpus. For instance, say you need to plan a corpus for your child’s higher education. Your child is now 1 year old, and you will need the funds when your child is 18. In this case, your investment horizon would be 17 years.
Costs
Some investment avenues might have costs associated with them. For instance, mutual funds have a total expense ratio, stocks have a brokerage fee, etc. Check the costs as they affect the overall returns.
Liquidity
Check the liquidity of the investment avenue that you choose and ensure that it aligns with your liquidity needs. Some investment options might have a lock-in period during which withdrawals are not allowed. This affects liquidity.
Child's Choice
Assess your child’s aspirations. The fees for a medical degree might be different than that of an engineering or law degree. Knowing the child’s choice would help you plan the optimal corpus needed to fund their dreams.
Financial Discipline
Lastly, discipline is important when saving for your child. When you save in a disciplined manner, you can allow your savings to grow and create a decent corpus for the child. Avoid the temptation to withdraw from the corpus meant for your child’s future.
Tax Benefits of Child Investment Plans in India
Many child investment plans can give you tax-saving benefits too. Here’s a look at the tax benefits that are usually available -
Deduction under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961
Section 80C of the Income Tax Act of 1961 is available if you choose the old tax regime of filing returns. You can claim a deduction of up to ₹1.5 lakhs on eligible investments. Some of the eligible investment avenues that allow savings under Section 80C are as follows –
Premiums paid for life insurance policies
Public Provident Fund (PPF)
Equity Linked Savings Schemes (ELSS)
Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (SSY)
National Savings Certificates (NSC)
5-year fixed deposits, etc.
Not Having to Pay Tax on Profits
Some investment plans also offer tax-free profits. Common examples are as follows –
Maturity benefits received from a life insurance policy enjoy tax exemption under Section 10(10D) if the premiums are up to 10% of the sum assured and are up to ₹2.5 lakhs (for ULIPs) or ₹5 lakhs (for non-ULIP plans)
PPF offers tax-free interest income and maturity benefits
Under ELSS funds, returns up to ₹1.25 lakhs are tax-free.
Interest earned and the maturity proceeds from the SSY account are also tax-free.
Calculators to Plan Your Child’s Financial Future
There are different calculators that you can use to calculate how much you should save for your child’s future needs. Some of these calculators are as follows -
Child Education Planner
This calculator helps you plan for your child’s higher education. You can enter the corpus needed, time to attain the goal, existing investment, expected inflation rate, and estimated rate of return. By entering these parameters, you can find out how much savings are needed to build the desired corpus.
Marriage Expense Calculator
This calculator helps you plan a corpus for your child’s marriage. You can plan your savings depending on the amount of corpus needed, investment tenure, expected rate of return, and inflation.
Investment Calculator
The investment calculator can give you an estimate of the corpus that you can create based on how much you invest. You can enter the amount that you want to invest, investment frequency, investment tenure, and the type of investment that you want. Based on these parameters, the calculator can calculate the corpus that your investment would create.
Life Insurance Calculator
The life insurance calculator can help you find the optimal sum assured of a term insurance plan that can provide financial security to your family in your absence.
Mistakes to Avoid in a Child Investment Plan
When investing for your child’s future, here are some mistakes to avoid –
Do not save randomly. Create a well-planned investment plan and save in a disciplined manner.
Do not ignore your risk appetite when selecting investment avenues.
Do not invest blindly before understanding your financial needs and requirements.
Do not dip into the savings for unnecessary spending. Try to build the corpus in a disciplined manner.
Key Takeaways
Child investment plans can help you save for your child’s future over time.
There are different types of child investment plans, and each plan has its own features, benefits, risk profile, investment horizon, and tax implications.
It is better to start saving for your child’s future from an early age so that you have time for compounding returns to grow your corpus.
You can use different types of calculators to find out how much to save to create the desired corpus or the corpus that would be needed for the important milestones of your child’s life.
Conclusion
As you plan to give the best things to your children, it is recommended to start saving early. You can select the available child investment plans based on your financial goals and investment strategy and build a corpus for your child’s secured future. It is also important to understand the tax implications of different investment avenues so that you can choose tax-saving options to create the desired corpus for your child. So, start early, save in a disciplined manner and give your children the gift of financial security to fulfil their dreams.
FAQs
Can I withdraw funds from a child investment plan before maturity?
Withdrawal depends on the type of child investment plan that you choose. Some investment plans have a lock-in period or do not allow premature withdrawals, while others offer easy withdrawals. So, check the withdrawal terms of the child investment plan you have selected.
Can a minor be chosen as a nominee in a savings plan for children?
A minor can be chosen as a nominee in a savings plan. However, in such cases, another adult individual should be appointed to receive the proceeds on behalf of the minor nominee if the investor passes away while the nominee is still a minor.
Can I avail of a loan against child investment plans?
The availability of loans depends on the type of child investment plans selected. Life insurance, endowment and money back plans offer policy loans. In the case of other types of investment plans, like FDs, stocks, mutual funds, etc., there’s a facility of pledging the securities as collateral and taking a loan against their value.
Disclaimer
IN THIS POLICY, THE INVESTMENT RISK IN INVESTMENT PORTFOLIO IS BORNE BY THE POLICYHOLDER
The Unit Linked Insurance Products do not offer any liquidity during the first five years of the contract. The policyholder will not be able to surrender or withdraw the monies invested in Unit Linked Insurance Products completely or partially till the end of the fifth year.
ULIPs are different from the traditional insurance products and are subject to the risk factors. The premium paid in ULIPs are subject to investment risks associated with capital markets and the NAVs of the units may go up or down based on the performance of fund and factors influencing the capital market and the insured is responsible for his/her decisions. Please know the associated risks and the applicable charges, from your Insurance agent or the Intermediary or policy document issued by the insurance company. The various funds offered under this contract are the names of the funds and do not in any way indicate the quality of these plans, their future prospects and returns.
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. Over a period of time, this can help you earn exponential returns on your savings. When you start early, you can give your investment time to grow, and compounding can help enhance the returns. 2. Birth or Early Childhood
You can also start investing after your child is born or when your child is young. This would give you time to save up a corpus for your child’s higher education or marriage. 3. Stable Financial Situation
Assess your finances, too, when starting investments for your child. Saving is feasible when your financial situation is stable so that the saving doesn’t affect your budget.

1. Rising Education Costs
Education costs are rising steadily. You would need to create an optimal fund for your child’s higher education, whether in India or abroad. The plan continues till maturity and pays the maturity benefit, which can help the child meet their financial needs. 3. Corpus creation
Child investment plans can help you plan in ahead and save up for your child’s financial future. The returns that you get from these plans can help in creating a corpus that can fund your child’s higher education or marriage. 4. Compounding Benefits
Child investment plans that offer compounded returns can help grow your savings exponentially over time. You can create an optimal corpus with the benefit of compounding to meet the costs of education or marriage.
Features of Child Based Investment Plans
1. Investment and Financial Protection
2. Sum Received Upon Maturity
3. Partial Withdrawals
4. Emergency Reserves
How Much to Invest Monthly for Your Child’s Future Goals?
The investment amount depends on your savings and the corpus that you need for your child’s future. Here are some instances showing how much you need to save for different combinations of interest rate, investment period, and the corpus needed –
Corpus needed Investment tenure Expected returns Savings required per month ₹50 lakhs 15 years 8% p.a. ₹14,450 ₹75 lakhs 15 years 10% p.a. ₹18,095 ₹1 crore 15 years 12% p.a. ₹20,016
Select the Best Investment Plan for Your Child
When choosing an investment plan for your child, here are some factors to consider -
1. Risk Profile
Assess your risk-taking ability so that you can choose a suitable investment avenue. For instance, if you don’t mind taking investment risks, you can invest in market-linked avenues like mutual funds, ULIPs, stocks, etc. However, if you do not like taking risks, you can choose fixed-income avenues like fixed deposits, recurring deposits, etc. 2. Investment Horizon
Next, consider your investment horizon, i.e., the period you have to invest after which you would need the corpus. For instance, say you need to plan a corpus for your child’s higher education. Your child is now 1 year old, and you will need the funds when your child is 18. In this case, your investment horizon would be 17 years. 3. Costs
Some investment avenues might have costs associated with them. For instance, mutual funds have a total expense ratio, stocks have a brokerage fee, etc. Check the costs as they affect the overall returns. 4. Liquidity
Check the liquidity of the investment avenue that you choose and ensure that it aligns with your liquidity needs. Some investment options might have a lock-in period during which withdrawals are not allowed. This affects liquidity. 5. Child's Choice
Assess your child’s aspirations. The fees for a medical degree might be different than that of an engineering or law degree. Knowing the child’s choice would help you plan the optimal corpus needed to fund their dreams. 6. Financial Discipline
Lastly, discipline is important when saving for your child. When you save in a disciplined manner, you can allow your savings to grow and create a decent corpus for the child. Avoid the temptation to withdraw from the corpus meant for your child’s future.
Tax Benefits of Child Investment Plans in India
Many child investment plans can give you tax-saving benefits too. Here’s a look at the tax benefits that are usually available -
1. Deduction under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 19611
Section 80C of the Income Tax Act of 1961 is available if you choose the old tax regime of filing returns. You can claim a deduction of up to ₹1.5 lakhs on eligible investments. Some of the eligible investment avenues that allow savings under Section 80C are as follows –
o Premiums paid for life insurance policies o Public Provident Fund (PPF) o Equity Linked Savings Schemes (ELSS) o Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (SSY) o National Savings Certificates (NSC) o 5-year fixed deposits, etc.
2. Not Having to Pay Tax on amount received from the investment
Some investment plans also offer tax-free profits. Common examples are as follows –
o Maturity benefits received from a life insurance policy enjoy tax exemption under
Section 10(10D) if the premiums are up to ₹2.5 lakhs (for ULIP policies bought on or after 1st February 2021) o PPF offers tax-free interest income o Under ELSS funds, returns up to ₹1.25 lakhs are tax-free3. o Interest earned and the maturity proceeds from the SSY account are also tax-free4.
Calculators to Plan Your Child’s Financial Future
There are different calculators that you can use to calculate how much you should save for your child’s future needs. Some of these calculators are as follows -
1. Child Education Planner
This calculator helps you plan for your child’s higher education. You have to enter parameters like the corpus needed, time to attain the goal, existing investment, , estimated rate of return. By entering these parameters, you can find out how much savings are needed to build the desired corpus. 2. Marriage Expense Calculator
This calculator helps you plan a corpus for your child’s marriage. You can plan your savings depending on the amount of corpus needed, investment tenure, expected rate of return. 3. Investment Calculator
The investment calculator can give you an estimate of the corpus that you can create based on how much you invest. You can enter the amount that you want to invest, investment frequency, investment tenure, and the type of investment that you want. Based on these parameters, the calculator can calculate the corpus that your investment would create. 4. Life Insurance Calculator
The life insurance calculator can help you find the optimal sum assured of a life insurance plan that can provide financial security to your family in your absence.
Mistakes to Avoid in a Child Investment Plan
When investing for your child’s future, here are some mistakes to avoid –
1. Do not save randomly. Create a well-planned investment plan and save in a disciplined
manner. 2. Do not ignore your risk appetite when selecting investment avenues. 3. Do not invest blindly before understanding your financial needs and requirements. 4. Do not dip into the savings for unnecessary spending. Try to build the corpus in a disciplined
manner. Key Takeaways
1. Child investment plans can help you save for your child’s future over time. 2. There are different types of child investment plans, and each plan has its own features,
benefits, risk profile, investment horizon, and tax implications. 3. It is better to start saving for your child’s future from an early age so that you have time for
compounding returns to grow your corpus. 4. You can use different types of calculators to find out how much to save to create the desired
corpus or the corpus that would be needed for the important milestones of your child’s life.

As you plan to give the best things to your children, it is recommended to start saving early. You can select the available child investment plans based on your financial goals and investment strategy and build a corpus for your child’s secured future. It is also important to understand the tax implications of different investment avenues so that you can choose tax-saving options to create the desired corpus for your child. So, start early, save in a disciplined manner and give your children the gift of financial security to fulfil their dreams.

1. Can I withdraw funds from a child investment plan before maturity?
Withdrawal depends on the type of child investment plan that you choose. Some investment plans have a lock-in period or do not allow premature withdrawals, while others offer easy withdrawals. In the case of other types of investment plans, like FDs, stocks, mutual funds, etc., there’s a facility of pledging the securities as collateral and taking a loan against their value.

changes.word_original: Best Investment Options for Your Child’s Future
changes.pdf_final: Investment Options for Your Child’s Future

changes.word_original: Called ULIPs in short, unit-linked plans are life insurance plans which combine the dual benefits of insurance coverage and returns that are linked to market performance.
changes.pdf_final: Called ULIPs in short, unit-linked insurance plans are life insurance plans which combine the dual benefits of life insurance coverage and market linked returns.

changes.word_original: A part of the premium that you pay for the ULIP is used to provide insurance coverage, while the other part is invested in a market-linked fund.
changes.pdf_final: A part of the premium that you pay for the ULIP is used to provide life insurance coverage, while the other part is invested in a market-linked funds.

changes.word_original: You also get the flexibility of switching and partial withdrawals to manage your investment.
changes.pdf_final: You also get the flexibility of switching and partial withdrawals to manage your investment. 2.

changes.word_original: You can choose child insurance plans that offer the added protection of a premium waiver rider.
changes.pdf_final: You can choose child insurance plans that offer the added protection of a waiver of premium rider.

changes.word_original: This rider waives the premiums if the parent passes away during the policy tenure.
changes.pdf_final: This rider waives the premiums in conditions like the parent passing away during the policy tenure.

changes.word_original: However, the policy continues and helps in creating the corpus even in the parents’ absence.
changes.pdf_final: However, the policy continues and helps in creating the corpus even in the parents’ absence. 3.

changes.word_original: Systematic Investment Plans (SIPs)
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Systematic Investment Plans (SIPs) are modes of investing in mutual funds.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: SIPs allow you to invest systematically and in a disciplined manner without the need to time the market.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Moreover, they are an affordable way to start building a corpus for your child over time.
changes.summary: Sentence from Word removed in PDF

changes.word_original: Under recurring deposits, you invest a specific sum of money at regular intervals and up to a specified tenure.
changes.pdf_final: Under recurring deposits, you invest a specific sum of money at regular intervals and up to a specified tenure. 4.

changes.word_original: The interest rate is fixed and determined by the government from time to time.
changes.pdf_final: The interest rate is fixed and determined by the government from time to time. 5.

changes.word_original: You can also invest in physical or digital gold to save up for your child’s future.
changes.pdf_final: You can also consider physical or digital gold as an investment to save up for your child’s future.

changes.word_original: Moreover, gold as an asset can also be used for its resale or traditional value.
changes.pdf_final: Moreover, gold as an asset can also be used for its resale or traditional value. 6.

changes.word_original: The maximum deposit is limited to ₹1.5 lakhs, and PPFs come with a tenure of 15 years,s which can be increased in blocks of 5 years.
changes.pdf_final: The maximum deposit is limited to ₹1.5 lakhs, and PPFs come with a tenure of 15 years,s which can be increased in blocks of 5 years. 7.

changes.word_original: Many bonds are also listed on the stock exchange, offering liquidity.
changes.pdf_final: Many bonds are also listed on the stock exchange, offering liquidity. 8.

changes.word_original: Moreover, you can let out the property and generate a regular source of income through rent.
changes.pdf_final: Moreover, you can let out the property and generate a regular source of income through rent. 9.

changes.pdf_final: You can choose the Systematic Investment Plans (SIPs) mode for considering mutual funds.
SIPs allow you to invest systematically and in a disciplined manner without the need to time the market.
Moreover, they are an affordable way to start building a corpus for your child over time.

changes.word_original: Then, you can invest in the child plan in a lump sum or systematically, depending on the type of investment avenue selected and your savings preference.
changes.pdf_final: Then, you can invest in the child savings plan in a lump sum or systematically, depending on the type of investment avenue selected and your savings preference.

changes.word_original: When you start early, you can give your investment time to grow, and compounding can help enhance the returns.
changes.pdf_final: When you start early, you can give your investment time to grow, and compounding can help enhance the returns. 2.

changes.word_original: This would give you time to save up a corpus for your child’s higher education or marriage.
changes.pdf_final: This would give you time to save up a corpus for your child’s higher education or marriage. 3.

changes.word_original: The different types of child investment plans can generate returns on your savings and help you create funds for your child’s education.
changes.pdf_final: The different types of child investment plans can generate returns on your savings and help you create funds for your child’s education. 2.

changes.word_original: Child plans usually offer the premium waiver benefit feature.
changes.pdf_final: Child plans usually offer the waiver of premium feature either as an inbuilt benefit or as a rider.

changes.word_original: This benefit secures the child’s future even if the parent passes away during the policy tenure.
changes.pdf_final: This benefit secures the child’s future in circumstances like the parent passing away during the policy tenure.

changes.word_original: The plan continues till maturity and pays the maturity benefit, which can help the child meet their financial needs.
changes.pdf_final: The plan continues till maturity and pays the maturity benefit, which can help the child meet their financial needs. 3.
